hindiTranslation: अति कड़वे, अति खट्टे, अति नमकीन, अति गरम, अति तीखे, अति रूखे और अति दाहकारक आहार अर्थात् भोजनके पदार्थ राजस मनुष्यको प्रिय होते हैं, जो कि दुःख, शोक और रोगोंको देनेवाले हैं ॥
englishTranslation: Bitter, sour, salty, overly hot, pungent, dry, and burning foods are dear to the passionate person. They bring pain, grief, and illness.
essence: What excites the senses can quietly produce suffering.
speaker: Krishna
simpleMeaning: Foods that are too sharp, hot, dry, or harsh attract a restless, passionate nature, but they lead to pain, grief, and illness.
simpleInsight: What feels exciting in the moment can quietly wear you down afterward.
detailedMeaning: On the battlefield, Krishna is teaching Arjuna how the qualities of food reveal the qualities of mind. After describing sattvic food, he now shows the rajasic pattern: what excites the senses often disturbs the person who craves intensity.
modernRelevance: You reach for another extra-spicy, late-night meal because your nerves want a hit, not balance. The body feels it later — in sleep, mood, and energy.
translationLiteral: कटु (bitter) / अम्ल (sour) / लवण (salty) / अत्युष्ण (overly hot) / तीक्ष्ण (pungent) / रूक्ष (dry) / विदाहिनः (burning) / आहाराः (foods) / राजसस्य (of the passionate person) / इष्टाः (dear) / दुःख (pain) / शोक (grief) / आमय (illness) / प्रदाः (giving)
insight: It shifts attention from taste alone to the mental state that chooses the food and the result it creates.
